title: El Caleuche: The Ghost Ship of Chiloé
summary: A brilliantly lit ghost ship crewed by witches and demons sails the canals of southern Chile, luring sailors to their doom.
full_text:
In the cold, misty channels of the Chiloé Archipelago in southern Chile, where the Pacific Ocean crashes against islands covered in ancient forests, there is a ship that has been sailing for centuries. It appears suddenly, brilliantly illuminated, its decks crowded with laughing figures. It is El Caleuche, the ghost ship of Chilote mythology, and its appearance is an omen of death.

El Caleuche is a three-masted schooner painted white, glowing with a light that seems to come from inside the hull itself. It sails at night, moving against the wind, its sails full even in a dead calm. Music pours from its decks — the sound of violins, harps, and guitars playing melodies that no human has ever composed.

The crew of the Caleuche are not ghosts. They are warlocks and witches who sold their souls for wealth and power. By day, they live normal lives in the villages of Chiloé. By night, they board the Caleuche to feast, dance, and plot their dark schemes. The ship is their meeting place, their floating palace of pleasure.

Any sailor who sees the Caleuche is in danger. If he shows fear, the ship will chase him across the sea, driving him onto the rocks. If he tries to approach, the music will enchant him, drawing him aboard. Once on the Caleuche, he will be forced to join the crew, his soul trapped forever on the ghost ship.

The only way to survive an encounter with the Caleuche is to pretend not to see it. If you look at the ship and immediately look away, the Caleuche will not be able to trap you. The witches and warlocks can only take those who acknowledge their existence.

Some versions of the legend say the Caleuche can transform itself into a rock or a floating log to hide from pursuers. Others say it can sail both on the surface and underwater, disappearing into the depths when threatened. The ship is as elusive as it is terrifying.

The legend of the Caleuche may have been inspired by the ships of the Dutch corsairs who raided the Chilean coast in the 17th century. Or it may reflect the disappearance of the Spanish explorer Alonso de Ercilla's expedition. But for the people of Chiloé, the Caleuche is real. It is the ghost that haunts their islands, the reminder that the sea is full of mysteries and that some ships sail forever without ever reaching port.
moral: The sea holds secrets that are not meant to be seen; some mysteries are best ignored.

title: El Trauco: The Forest Dwarf of Chiloé
summary: A grotesque dwarf-like creature with powers of irresistible seduction haunts the forests of Chiloé.
full_text:
Deep in the forests of Chiloé, where the alerce trees grow so tall that their tops disappear into the mist and the ferns carpet the ground in emerald green, there lives a creature of extraordinary power. He is El Trauco, a dwarf-like being who is one of the most feared figures in Chilote folklore.

El Trauco is small and ugly. He stands no more than three feet tall, with a twisted body, long arms, and legs so short that he seems to crawl rather than walk. His face is hideous — wrinkled, with a huge nose and small, glittering eyes. He wears a hat made of vines and clothes of bark, and he carries a staff called the pahueldún, a magical stick carved from the wood of the alerce tree.

Despite his grotesque appearance, El Trauco has an irresistible power. His gaze can seduce any woman. From a distance, he makes eye contact, and the woman is overcome with desire. She drops whatever she is carrying and walks into the forest, following the Trauco to his hidden cave. There, he seduces her, and she returns to her village hours later with no memory of what happened.

Women who become pregnant after meeting the Trauco are not shamed. In Chilote society, a child fathered by the Trauco is considered blessed — born with special powers, perhaps the ability to see spirits or to heal with herbs. The Trauco provides a socially acceptable explanation for unexpected pregnancies, a supernatural reason that does not require punishment or shame.

But the Trauco is not only a seducer. He is also a punisher. Those who disrespect the forest, who cut trees without permission, or who harm the animals of Chiloé, will feel the Trauco's wrath. He uses his pahueldún to cripple them, striking their legs so that they can never walk properly again.

The Trauco is still believed in by many rural Chilotes. They know to avoid certain parts of the forest, especially at dusk. Women carry scissors in their pockets, believing that the metal repels the Trauco. Men who go into the forest recite a prayer to San Antonio, asking for protection from the dwarf's power.

The Trauco represents the untamed power of the forests of southern Chile. He is the guardian of the wild places, the seducer who protects the trees, and the explanation for the mysteries that still linger in the ancient woods of Chiloé.
moral: The wilderness has its own laws; enter with respect or face the consequences.
